mnemonics.title: Remember Translation Matrix
mnemonics.content: Translation matrix: 'Identity with translation in the third column' - Think: T = I + [tx, ty] in last column

mnemonics.title: Remember Rotation Matrix
mnemonics.content: Rotation: cos-sin on diagonal, sin+cos on anti-diagonal. Cos-Sin, Sin+Cos: Like reading clockwise starting top-left gives cos(θ), -sin(θ), sin(θ), cos(θ)

mnemonics.title: Remember Scaling
mnemonics.content: Scaling: 'Scale goes on the diagonal, rest are zeros' - Just like identity but replace 1s with scale factors.

mnemonics.title: Remember Transformation Order
mnemonics.content: TRAP: Transform Right-to-Left Apply Programming - Composite is T × R × Point (rightmost applies first)

acronyms.usage: Used to represent nD points in n+1 dimensions for unified transformation matrices
The standard reference frame for transformations
Coordinate system attached to an object for local transformations
3×3 matrix representing all 2D affine transformations in a unified form

visualTips.visualization: Think of identity matrix with translation values filling the 'elevator' in the third column, moving points to new floor (y-value) and room (x-value)
Picture a clock: cos at 12 and 6 o'clock positions (diagonal), sin and -sin at 3 and 9 o'clock (anti-diagonal). Counterclockwise rotation follows the clock hands direction.
The diagonal elements are like 'stretchy' rubber bands - pull them to stretch in x or y direction, all other elements stay at zero like empty space.
Think of (x,y,w) as (x/w, y/w) - like a fraction. When w=1, it's in 'simplest form'. When w changes, it creates perspective like a camera lens.
Stack matrices like building blocks - each transformation adds one block. Rightmost block touches the point first (gets applied first).

analogies.analogy: Like giving directions using both street address AND landmark reference - the w component adds that extra reference frame enabling transformations to 'find' the point.
Before homogeneous coords, translation was like 'push' operations (addition). Now it's like having a universal remote that can do EVERYTHING with just button presses (matrix multiplications).
Like following a recipe - each ingredient (matrix) is added in specific order. Adding flour then eggs gives different result than eggs then flour, even with same ingredients.

A direction (vector) is like an arrow - it points somewhere but has no specific location. The w=0 makes it 'immune' to translation, just like how moving the entire coordinate system doesn't change which way an arrow points.
